title: Martin Scorsese

infobox.value: Martin Charles Scorsese   (1942-11-17) November 17, 1942 (age 83)  [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), U.S.
[New York University](/wiki/New_York_University "New York University") ([BA](/wiki/Bachelor_of_Arts "Bachelor of Arts"), [MA](/wiki/Master_of_Arts "Master of Arts"))

* Laraine Marie Brennan  ​  ​ (m. 1965; div. 1971)​ * [Julia Cameron](/wiki/Julia_Cameron "Julia Cameron")  ​  ​ (m. 1976; div. 1977)​ * [Isabella Rossellini](/wiki/Isabella_Rossellini "Isabella Rossellini")  ​  ​ (m. 1979; div. 1982)​ * [Barbara De Fina](/wiki/Barbara_De_Fina "Barbara De Fina")  ​  ​ (m. 1985; div. 1991)​ * Helen Schermerhorn Morris  ​  ​ (m. 1999)​
[Full list](/wiki/List_of_awards_and_nominations_received_by_Martin_Scorsese "List of awards and nominations received by Martin Scorsese")

([/skɔːrˈsɛsi/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*skor-SESS-ee*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key");[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) Italian: [[skorˈseːze, -se]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian"); born November 17, 1942) is an American filmmaker. One of the major figures of the [New Hollywood](/wiki/New_Hollywood "New Hollywood") era, he is widely considered one of the greatest and most influential directors in the history of cinema. He has received [many accolades](/wiki/List_of_awards_and_nominations_received_by_Martin_Scorsese "List of awards and nominations received by Martin Scorsese"), including an [Academy Award](/wiki/Academy_Award "Academy Award"), four [BAFTA Awards](/wiki/BAFTA_Awards "BAFTA Awards"), three [Emmy Awards](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Awards "Primetime Emmy Awards"), a [Grammy Award](/wiki/Grammy_Award "Grammy Award"), and three [Golden Globe Awards](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Awards "Golden Globe Awards"). He has been honored with the [AFI Life Achievement Award](/wiki/AFI_Life_Achievement_Award "AFI Life Achievement Award") in 1997, the [Film Society of Lincoln Center](/wiki/Film_Society_of_Lincoln_Center "Film Society of Lincoln Center") tribute in 1998, the [Kennedy Center Honor](/wiki/Kennedy_Center_Honor "Kennedy Center Honor") in 2007, the [Cecil B. DeMille Award](/wiki/Cecil_B._DeMille_Award "Cecil B. DeMille Award") in 2010, and the [BAFTA Fellowship](/wiki/BAFTA_Fellowship "BAFTA Fellowship") in 2012. Four of his films have been inducted into the [National Film Registry](/wiki/National_Film_Registry "National Film Registry") by the [Library of Congress](/wiki/Library_of_Congress "Library of Congress") as "culturally, historically or aesthetically significant".

**Martin Charles Scorsese** ([/skɔːrˈsɛsi/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*skor-SESS-ee*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key");[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) Italian: [[skorˈseːze, -se]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian"); born November 17, 1942) is an American filmmaker. One of the major figures of the [New Hollywood](/wiki/New_Hollywood "New Hollywood") era, he is widely considered one of the greatest and most influential directors in the history of cinema. He has received [many accolades](/wiki/List_of_awards_and_nominations_received_by_Martin_Scorsese "List of awards and nominations received by Martin Scorsese"), including an [Academy Award](/wiki/Academy_Award "Academy Award"), four [BAFTA Awards](/wiki/BAFTA_Awards "BAFTA Awards"), three [Emmy Awards](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Awards "Primetime Emmy Awards"), a [Grammy Award](/wiki/Grammy_Award "Grammy Award"), and three [Golden Globe Awards](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Awards "Golden Globe Awards"). He has been honored with the [AFI Life Achievement Award](/wiki/AFI_Life_Achievement_Award "AFI Life Achievement Award") in 1997, the [Film Society of Lincoln Center](/wiki/Film_Society_of_Lincoln_Center "Film Society of Lincoln Center") tribute in 1998, the [Kennedy Center Honor](/wiki/Kennedy_Center_Honor "Kennedy Center Honor") in 2007, the [Cecil B. DeMille Award](/wiki/Cecil_B._DeMille_Award "Cecil B. DeMille Award") in 2010, and the [BAFTA Fellowship](/wiki/BAFTA_Fellowship "BAFTA Fellowship") in 2012. Four of his films have been inducted into the [National Film Registry](/wiki/National_Film_Registry "National Film Registry") by the [Library of Congress](/wiki/Library_of_Congress "Library of Congress") as "culturally, historically or aesthetically significant".

Scorsese received a Master of Arts degree from [New York University](/wiki/New_York_University "New York University")'s [Steinhardt School of Culture, Education, and Human Development](/wiki/Steinhardt_School_of_Culture,_Education,_and_Human_Development "Steinhardt School of Culture, Education, and Human Development") in 1968. His directorial debut, *[Who's That Knocking at My Door](/wiki/Who%27s_That_Knocking_at_My_Door "Who's That Knocking at My Door")* (1967), was accepted into the [Chicago Film Festival](/wiki/Chicago_Film_Festival "Chicago Film Festival"). In the 1970s and 1980s, [Scorsese's films](/wiki/Martin_Scorsese_filmography "Martin Scorsese filmography"), much influenced by his [Italian-American](/wiki/Italian-American "Italian-American") background and upbringing in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), centered on macho-posturing men and explore crime, machismo, [nihilism](/wiki/Nihilism "Nihilism") and [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic") concepts of guilt and redemption.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) His trademark styles of extensive use of [slow motion](/wiki/Slow_motion "Slow motion") and [freeze frames](/wiki/Freeze-frame_shot "Freeze-frame shot"), voice-over [narration](/wiki/Narration "Narration"), graphic depictions of extreme [violence](/wiki/Violence "Violence") and liberal use of [profanity](/wiki/Profanity "Profanity") were first shown in *[Mean Streets](/wiki/Mean_Streets "Mean Streets")* (1973).

Scorsese won the [Palme d'Or](/wiki/Palme_d%27Or "Palme d'Or") at [Cannes](/wiki/1976_Cannes_Film_Festival "1976 Cannes Film Festival") with *[Taxi Driver](/wiki/Taxi_Driver "Taxi Driver")* (1976), which starred [Robert De Niro](/wiki/Robert_De_Niro "Robert De Niro") as a disturbed Vietnam veteran. De Niro became [associated with Scorsese through eight more films](/wiki/Martin_Scorsese_and_Robert_De_Niro "Martin Scorsese and Robert De Niro") including [*New York, New York*](/wiki/New_York,_New_York_(1977_film) "New York, New York (1977 film)") (1977), *[Raging Bull](/wiki/Raging_Bull "Raging Bull")* (1980), *[The King of Comedy](/wiki/The_King_of_Comedy_(1982_film) "The King of Comedy (1982 film)")* (1982), *[Goodfellas](/wiki/Goodfellas "Goodfellas")* (1990), *[Cape Fear](/wiki/Cape_Fear_(1991_film) "Cape Fear (1991 film)")* (1991), *[Casino](/wiki/Casino_(1995_film) "Casino (1995 film)")* (1995) and *[The Irishman](/wiki/The_Irishman "The Irishman")* (2019). In the following decades, he garnered box office success with a [series of collaborations](/wiki/Martin_Scorsese_and_Leonardo_DiCaprio "Martin Scorsese and Leonardo DiCaprio") with [Leonardo DiCaprio](/wiki/Leonardo_DiCaprio "Leonardo DiCaprio"), including *[Gangs of New York](/wiki/Gangs_of_New_York "Gangs of New York")* (2002), *[The Aviator](/wiki/The_Aviator_(2004_film) "The Aviator (2004 film)")* (2004), *[The Departed](/wiki/The_Departed "The Departed")* (2006), *[Shutter Island](/wiki/Shutter_Island_(film) "Shutter Island (film)")* (2010), and *[The Wolf of Wall Street](/wiki/The_Wolf_of_Wall_Street_(2013_film) "The Wolf of Wall Street (2013 film)")* (2013). He worked with both De Niro and DiCaprio on *[Killers of the Flower Moon](/wiki/Killers_of_the_Flower_Moon "Killers of the Flower Moon")* (2023). He also directed [*After Hours*](/wiki/After_Hours_(film) "After Hours (film)") (1985), *[The Color of Money](/wiki/The_Color_of_Money "The Color of Money")* (1986), *[The Last Temptation of Christ](/wiki/The_Last_Temptation_of_Christ_(film) "The Last Temptation of Christ (film)")* (1988), *[The Age of Innocence](/wiki/The_Age_of_Innocence_(1993_film) "The Age of Innocence (1993 film)")* (1993), *[Kundun](/wiki/Kundun "Kundun")* (1997), *[Hugo](/wiki/Hugo_(film) "Hugo (film)")* (2011), and *[Silence](/wiki/Silence_(2016_film) "Silence (2016 film)")* (2016).

On television, he has directed episodes for the [HBO](/wiki/HBO "HBO") series *[Boardwalk Empire](/wiki/Boardwalk_Empire "Boardwalk Empire")* (2010–2014) and *[Vinyl](/wiki/Vinyl_(TV_series) "Vinyl (TV series)")* (2016), as well as the HBO documentary *[Public Speaking](/wiki/Public_Speaking_(film) "Public Speaking (film)")* (2010) and the [Netflix](/wiki/Netflix "Netflix") [docu-series](/wiki/Docu-series "Docu-series") *[Pretend It's a City](/wiki/Pretend_It%27s_a_City "Pretend It's a City")* (2021). He has also directed several rock documentaries including *[The Last Waltz](/wiki/The_Last_Waltz "The Last Waltz")* (1978), *[No Direction Home](/wiki/No_Direction_Home "No Direction Home")* (2005), and *[Shine a Light](/wiki/Shine_a_Light_(film) "Shine a Light (film)")* (2008). He has explored film history in the documentaries *[A Personal Journey with Martin Scorsese Through American Movies](/wiki/A_Personal_Journey_with_Martin_Scorsese_Through_American_Movies "A Personal Journey with Martin Scorsese Through American Movies")* (1995) and *[My Voyage to Italy](/wiki/My_Voyage_to_Italy "My Voyage to Italy")* (1999).[[5]](#cite_note-5) An advocate for [film preservation](/wiki/Film_preservation "Film preservation") and [restoration](/wiki/Film_restoration "Film restoration"), he has founded three nonprofit organizations: [The Film Foundation](/wiki/The_Film_Foundation "The Film Foundation") in 1990, the [World Cinema Foundation](/wiki/World_Cinema_Foundation "World Cinema Foundation") in 2007 and the African Film Heritage Project in 2017.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Early life and education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Martin_Scorsese&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life and education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Cuccia-Scorsese-Deseta.jpg/330px-Cuccia-Scorsese-Deseta.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cuccia-Scorsese-Deseta.jpg)

From left to right: [Salvo Cuccia](/wiki/Salvo_Cuccia "Salvo Cuccia"), Scorsese, and [Vittorio De Seta](/wiki/Vittorio_De_Seta "Vittorio De Seta") at the 2005 [Tribeca Film Festival](/wiki/Tribeca_Film_Festival "Tribeca Film Festival")

Martin Charles Scorsese was born in the [Flushing](/wiki/Flushing,_Queens "Flushing, Queens") neighborhood of New York City's Queens borough on November 17, 1942.[[7]](#cite_note-NYTbio-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) He grew up in the [Little Italy](/wiki/Little_Italy,_Manhattan "Little Italy, Manhattan") neighborhood of the city's Manhattan borough.[[9]](#cite_note-9) Both of his parents, [Catherine Scorsese](/wiki/Catherine_Scorsese "Catherine Scorsese") (née Cappa) and [Charles Scorsese](/wiki/Charles_Scorsese "Charles Scorsese"), worked in the [Garment District](/wiki/Garment_District,_Manhattan "Garment District, Manhattan"). Charles was a clothes presser and actor, while Catherine was a seamstress and an actress.[[10]](#cite_note-10) All four of Scorsese's grandparents were Italian immigrants from [Sicily](/wiki/Sicily "Sicily"), hailing from [Polizzi Generosa](/wiki/Polizzi_Generosa "Polizzi Generosa") on his father's side and [Ciminna](/wiki/Ciminna "Ciminna") on his mother's side.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) The original surname of the family was Scozzese, meaning "Scot" or "Scottish" in Italian, and was changed to Scorsese because of a transcription error.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Scorsese was raised in a predominantly [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic") environment.[[7]](#cite_note-NYTbio-7) As a boy, he had [asthma](/wiki/Asthma "Asthma") and could not play sports or take part in any activities with other children, so his parents and his older brother would often take him to movie theaters; it was at this stage in his life that he developed a passion for cinema. He has spoken of the influence on him of [Powell and Pressburger](/wiki/Powell_and_Pressburger "Powell and Pressburger")'s *[Black Narcissus](/wiki/Black_Narcissus "Black Narcissus")* (1947) and [*The Red Shoes*](/wiki/The_Red_Shoes_(1948_film) "The Red Shoes (1948 film)") (1948).[[16]](#cite_note-DVD-16) As a teenager living in [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn"), he frequently commuted to [the Bronx](/wiki/The_Bronx "The Bronx") to rent Powell and Pressburger's film, [*The Tales of Hoffmann*](/wiki/The_Tales_of_Hoffmann_(film) "The Tales of Hoffmann (film)") (1951), from a store which had only one copy of the reel. He was one of only two people who regularly rented it; the other, [George A. Romero](/wiki/George_A._Romero "George A. Romero"), who then lived in that [borough](/wiki/Borough "Borough"), also became a director.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

Scorsese has named [Sabu](/wiki/Sabu_Dastagir "Sabu Dastagir") and [Victor Mature](/wiki/Victor_Mature "Victor Mature") as his favorite actors in his youth. He recalls his father taking him to see [Jean Renoir](/wiki/Jean_Renoir "Jean Renoir")'s [*The River*](/wiki/The_River_(1951_film) "The River (1951 film)") (1951) and being fascinated by its depiction of [India](/wiki/India "India"). He became "obsessed" with Renoir's *[La Grande Illusion](/wiki/La_Grande_Illusion "La Grande Illusion")* (1937) when it was rereleased.[[18]](#cite_note-18) He names [John Ford](/wiki/John_Ford "John Ford")'s *[The Quiet Man](/wiki/The_Quiet_Man "The Quiet Man")* (1952) and *[The Searchers](/wiki/The_Searchers "The Searchers")* (1956) as formative influences.[[19]](#cite_note-19) In a documentary on [Italian neorealism](/wiki/Italian_neorealism "Italian neorealism"), he commented on how [Roberto Rossellini](/wiki/Roberto_Rossellini "Roberto Rossellini")'s *[Rome, Open City](/wiki/Rome,_Open_City "Rome, Open City")* (1945) and [Vittorio De Sica](/wiki/Vittorio_De_Sica "Vittorio De Sica")'s *[Bicycle Thieves](/wiki/Bicycle_Thieves "Bicycle Thieves")* (1946) inspired him and influenced his view of his Sicilian roots. In his documentary *[Il Mio Viaggio in Italia](/wiki/Il_Mio_Viaggio_in_Italia "Il Mio Viaggio in Italia")* (*My Voyage to Italy*), Scorsese noted that the Sicilian episode of Rossellini's *[Paisà](/wiki/Pais%C3%A0 "Paisà")* (1946), which he first saw on television with his relatives who were themselves Sicilian immigrants, had a significant impact on his life.[[20]](#cite_note-hatchet-20) He remembers responding "very strongly" to [Alfred Hitchcock](/wiki/Alfred_Hitchcock "Alfred Hitchcock")'s [*Vertigo*](/wiki/Vertigo_(film) "Vertigo (film)") (1958).[[21]](#cite_note-21) He acknowledges owing a great debt to the [French New Wave](/wiki/French_New_Wave "French New Wave") and has stated that "the French New Wave has influenced all filmmakers who have worked since, whether they saw the films or not."[[22]](#cite_note-22) He has also cited the works of [Satyajit Ray](/wiki/Satyajit_Ray "Satyajit Ray"),[[20]](#cite_note-hatchet-20) [Ingmar Bergman](/wiki/Ingmar_Bergman "Ingmar Bergman"),[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24) [Andrzej Wajda](/wiki/Andrzej_Wajda "Andrzej Wajda"),[[25]](#cite_note-25) [Michelangelo Antonioni](/wiki/Michelangelo_Antonioni "Michelangelo Antonioni"),[[26]](#cite_note-26) [Federico Fellini](/wiki/Federico_Fellini "Federico Fellini"),[[27]](#cite_note-27) [Ishirō Honda](/wiki/Ishir%C5%8D_Honda "Ishirō Honda") and [Eiji Tsuburaya](/wiki/Eiji_Tsuburaya "Eiji Tsuburaya")[[28]](#cite_note-28) as major influences on his career. Although there was no habit of reading at home, towards the end of the 1950s, Scorsese began to approach literature, being marked in particular by [Fyodor Dostoevsky](/wiki/Fyodor_Dostoevsky "Fyodor Dostoevsky")'s *[Notes from Underground](/wiki/Notes_from_Underground "Notes from Underground")* (1864), [James Joyce](/wiki/James_Joyce "James Joyce")'s *[A Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man](/wiki/A_Portrait_of_the_Artist_as_a_Young_Man "A Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man")* (1916) and [Graham Greene](/wiki/Graham_Greene "Graham Greene")'s *[The Heart of the Matter](/wiki/The_Heart_of_the_Matter "The Heart of the Matter")* (1948).[[29]](#cite_note-29)

Scorsese attended the all-boys [Cardinal Hayes High School](/wiki/Cardinal_Hayes_High_School "Cardinal Hayes High School") in the Bronx, graduating in 1960.[[30]](#cite_note-ENCY-30) He had initially desired to become a [priest](/wiki/Priest "Priest"), attending a preparatory [seminary](/wiki/Seminary "Seminary"), but failed after the first year and was unable to attend [Fordham University](/wiki/Fordham_University "Fordham University").[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson201128-31)[[32]](#cite_note-Wooden_2016-32) This gave way to cinema and consequently Scorsese enrolled in [New York University](/wiki/New_York_University "New York University")'s [Washington Square College](/wiki/Washington_Square_College "Washington Square College") (now known as the [College of Arts and Science](/wiki/NYU_College_of_Arts_and_Science "NYU College of Arts and Science")), where he earned a bachelor's degree in English in 1964.[[30]](#cite_note-ENCY-30)[[33]](#cite_note-33) He went on to earn his Master of Arts from NYU's School of Education (now the [Steinhardt School of Culture, Education, and Human Development](/wiki/Steinhardt_School_of_Culture,_Education,_and_Human_Development "Steinhardt School of Culture, Education, and Human Development")) in 1968,[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35) a year after the school was founded.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Martin_Scorsese&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

### 1963–1972: Short films and feature debut

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Martin_Scorsese&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1963–1972: Short films and feature debut")]

While attending the Tisch School of the Arts, Scorsese made the short films *[What's a Nice Girl like You Doing in a Place like This?](/wiki/What%27s_a_Nice_Girl_like_You_Doing_in_a_Place_like_This%3F "What's a Nice Girl like You Doing in a Place like This?")* (1963) and *[It's Not Just You, Murray!](/wiki/It%27s_Not_Just_You,_Murray! "It's Not Just You, Murray!")* (1964). His most famous short of the period is the darkly comic *[The Big Shave](/wiki/The_Big_Shave "The Big Shave")* (1967), which features Peter Bernuth. The film is an indictment of America's involvement in [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War"), suggested by its alternative title *Viet '67*.[[37]](#cite_note-37) Scorsese has mentioned on several occasions that he was greatly inspired in his early days at New York University by [Armenian-American](/wiki/Armenian-American "Armenian-American")[[38]](#cite_note-38) film professor [Haig P. Manoogian](/wiki/Haig_P._Manoogian "Haig P. Manoogian"), calling his teachings “the most precious gift I have ever received”.[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41) Scorsese's first professional job was when he was at NYU; he was the assistant cameraman to cinematographer [Baird Bryant](/wiki/Baird_Bryant "Baird Bryant") on the [John G. Avildsen](/wiki/John_G._Avildsen "John G. Avildsen") directed short film *Smiles* (1964). Scorsese stated: "It was really important because they were filming on 35mm". He stated he was terrible at the job because he could not judge the distance of the focus. He also worked as a [gaffer](/wiki/Gaffer_(filmmaking) "Gaffer (filmmaking)") for [Albert and David Maysles](/wiki/Albert_and_David_Maysles "Albert and David Maysles") and as an editor for [CBS News](/wiki/CBS_News "CBS News"), the latter of whom offered him a full time position, but Scorsese declined due to his pursuit in film.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

In 1967, Scorsese made his first feature-length film, the black and white *I Call First*, later retitled *[Who's That Knocking at My Door](/wiki/Who%27s_That_Knocking_at_My_Door "Who's That Knocking at My Door")*, with his fellow students actor [Harvey Keitel](/wiki/Harvey_Keitel "Harvey Keitel") and editor [Thelma Schoonmaker](/wiki/Thelma_Schoonmaker "Thelma Schoonmaker"), both of whom were to become long-term collaborators. [Roger Ebert](/wiki/Roger_Ebert "Roger Ebert") saw the film at the 1967 [Chicago International Film Festival](/wiki/Chicago_International_Film_Festival "Chicago International Film Festival") and wrote, in Scorsese's first published review: "it brings together two opposing worlds of American cinema. On the one hand, there have been traditional films like [*Marty*](/wiki/Marty_(film) "Marty (film)"), [*View from the Bridge*](/wiki/A_View_from_the_Bridge_(film) "A View from the Bridge (film)"), *[On the Waterfront](/wiki/On_the_Waterfront "On the Waterfront")* and *[David and Lisa](/wiki/David_and_Lisa "David and Lisa")* -- all sincere attempts to function at the level where real lives are led and all suffering to some degree from their makers' romantic and idealistic ideas, about such lives. On the other hand, there have been experimental films from [Jonas Mekas](/wiki/Jonas_Mekas "Jonas Mekas"), [Shirley Clarke](/wiki/Shirley_Clarke "Shirley Clarke") and other pioneers of the New York underground. In [*The Connection*](/wiki/The_Connection_(1961_film) "The Connection (1961 film)"), [*Shadows*](/wiki/Shadows_(1959_film) "Shadows (1959 film)") and *[Guns of the Trees](/wiki/Guns_of_the_Trees "Guns of the Trees")*, they used improvised dialog and scenes and hidden and hand-held cameras in an attempt to capture the freshness of a spontaneous experience ... *I Call First* brings these two kinds of films together into a work that is absolutely genuine, artistically satisfying and technically comparable to the best films being made anywhere. I have no reservations in describing it as a great moment in American movies."[[43]](#cite_note-43)

Scorsese became friends with the influential "movie brats" of the 1970s: [Brian De Palma](/wiki/Brian_De_Palma "Brian De Palma"), [Francis Ford Coppola](/wiki/Francis_Ford_Coppola "Francis Ford Coppola"), [George Lucas](/wiki/George_Lucas "George Lucas") and [Steven Spielberg](/wiki/Steven_Spielberg "Steven Spielberg").[[44]](#cite_note-BANDB-44)[[45]](#cite_note-Adams1-45) It was De Palma who introduced Scorsese to [Robert De Niro](/wiki/Robert_De_Niro "Robert De Niro").[[44]](#cite_note-BANDB-44) During this period, Scorsese worked as the assistant director and one of the editors on [Michael Wadleigh](/wiki/Michael_Wadleigh "Michael Wadleigh")'s documentary *[Woodstock](/wiki/Woodstock_(film) "Woodstock (film)")* (1970) and met actor–director [John Cassavetes](/wiki/John_Cassavetes "John Cassavetes"), who became a close friend and mentor.[[46]](#cite_note-Thompson-46)

Scorsese met [Roger Corman](/wiki/Roger_Corman "Roger Corman") after coming to Hollywood to edit *Medicine Ball Caravan* and Corman, who had seen and liked *Who's That Knocking at My Door*, asked Scorsese to make a sequel to *[Bloody Mama](/wiki/Bloody_Mama "Bloody Mama")* (1970). This came to be *[Boxcar Bertha](/wiki/Boxcar_Bertha "Boxcar Bertha")* (1972).[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilson201133-47)[[46]](#cite_note-Thompson-46) It was Corman who taught Scorsese that entertaining films could be shot with very little money or time, preparing the young director well for the challenges to come. Following the film's release, Cassavetes encouraged Scorsese to make the films that he wanted to make, rather than someone else's projects.

### 1973–1989: Breakthrough and acclaim

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Martin_Scorsese&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1973–1989: Breakthrough and acclaim")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/Movie_trailer_screenshot_of_Robert_D_Niro_in_Mean_Streets_%281973%29.png/250px-Movie_trailer_screenshot_of_Robert_D_Niro_in_Mean_Streets_%281973%29.png)](/wiki/File:Movie_trailer_screenshot_of_Robert_D_Niro_in_Mean_Streets_(1973).png)

[Robert De Niro](/wiki/Robert_De_Niro "Robert De Niro") acted in his first collaboration with Scorsese in the crime drama *[Mean Streets](/wiki/Mean_Streets "Mean Streets")* (1973).

*[Mean Streets](/wiki/Mean_Streets "Mean Streets")* was a breakthrough for Scorsese, Keitel and [Robert De Niro](/wiki/Robert_De_Niro "Robert De Niro"). [Pauline Kael](/wiki/Pauline_Kael "Pauline Kael") wrote: "Martin Scorsese's *Mean Streets* is a true original of our time, a triumph of personal filmmaking. It has its own hallucinatory look; the characters live in the darkness of bars, with lighting and color just this shade of lurid ... It's about American life here and now, and it doesn't look like an American movie, or feel like one. If it were subtitled, we could hail a new European or South American talent — a new [Buñuel](/wiki/Luis_Bu%C3%B1uel "Luis Buñuel") steeped in [Verdi](/wiki/Verdi "Verdi"), perhaps."[[48]](#cite_note-48) By now the signature Scorsese style was in place: macho posturing, bloody violence, Catholic guilt and redemption, gritty New York locale (though the majority of *Mean Streets* was shot in Los Angeles), rapid-fire editing, and a soundtrack with contemporary music. Although the film was innovative, its wired atmosphere, edgy documentary style, and gritty street-level direction owed a debt to Cassavetes, [Samuel Fuller](/wiki/Samuel_Fuller "Samuel Fuller") and early [Jean-Luc Godard](/wiki/Jean-Luc_Godard "Jean-Luc Godard").[[49]](#cite_note-49) In 1974, actress [Ellen Burstyn](/wiki/Ellen_Burstyn "Ellen Burstyn") chose Scorsese to direct her in *[Alice Doesn't Live Here Anymore](/wiki/Alice_Doesn%27t_Live_Here_Anymore "Alice Doesn't Live Here Anymore")*, for which she won an Academy Award for Best Actress. Although well regarded, the film remains an anomaly in Scorsese's early career as it focuses on a central female character. Returning to Little Italy to explore his ethnic roots, Scorsese directed *[Italianamerican](/wiki/Italianamerican "Italianamerican")* (1974), a documentary featuring his parents Charles and Catherine Scorsese.

Scorsese followed with *[Taxi Driver](/wiki/Taxi_Driver "Taxi Driver")* in 1976, which depicted a [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War") veteran who takes the law into his own hands on New York's crime-ridden streets.[[50]](#cite_note-50) The film established him as an accomplished filmmaker and also brought attention to cinematographer [Michael Chapman](/wiki/Michael_Chapman_(cinematographer) "Michael Chapman (cinematographer)"), whose style tends towards high contrasts, strong colors, and complex camera movements. The film starred De Niro as the angry and alienated [Travis Bickle](/wiki/Travis_Bickle "Travis Bickle"), and co-starred [Jodie Foster](/wiki/Jodie_Foster "Jodie Foster") in a highly controversial role as an underage prostitute, with Harvey Keitel as her pimp.[[51]](#cite_note-51) *Taxi Driver* also marked the start of a series of collaborations between Scorsese and writer [Paul Schrader](/wiki/Paul_Schrader "Paul Schrader"), whose influences included the diary of would-be assassin [Arthur Bremer](/wiki/Arthur_Bremer "Arthur Bremer"), [John Ford](/wiki/John_Ford "John Ford")'s *[The Searchers](/wiki/The_Searchers "The Searchers")* (1956), and [Robert Bresson](/wiki/Robert_Bresson "Robert Bresson")'s [*Pickpocket*](/wiki/Pickpocket_(film) "Pickpocket (film)") (1959).[[52]](#cite_note-52) Already controversial upon its release, *Taxi Driver* hit the headlines again five years later, when [John Hinckley Jr.](/wiki/John_Hinckley_Jr. "John Hinckley Jr.") made an assassination attempt on then-president [Ronald Reagan](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan "Ronald Reagan"). He subsequently blamed his act on his obsession with Jodie Foster's *Taxi Driver* character (in the film, De Niro's character, Travis Bickle, makes an assassination attempt on a senator).[[53]](#cite_note-53)

*Taxi Driver* won the [Palme d'Or](/wiki/Palme_d%27Or "Palme d'Or") at the 1976 Cannes Film Festival,[[54]](#cite_note-54) also receiving four [Oscar](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards") nominations, including [Best Picture](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Picture "Academy Award for Best Picture"). The critical and financial success of *Taxi Driver* encouraged Scorsese to move ahead with his first big-budget project: the highly stylized musical *[New York, New York](/wiki/New_York,_New_York_(1977_film) "New York, New York (1977 film)")*. This tribute to Scorsese's home town and the classic Hollywood musical was a box-office failure. The film was the director's third collaboration with De Niro, co-starring with [Liza Minnelli](/wiki/Liza_Minnelli "Liza Minnelli"). The film is best remembered today for the title theme song, which was popularized by [Frank Sinatra](/wiki/Frank_Sinatra "Frank Sinatra"). Although possessing Scorsese's usual visual panache and stylistic bravura, many critics felt its enclosed studio-bound atmosphere left it leaden in comparison with his earlier work. Despite its weak reception, the film is regarded positively by some critics. [Richard Brody](/wiki/Richard_Brody "Richard Brody") wrote:

> For Scorsese, a lifelong cinephile, the essence of New York could be found in its depiction in classic Hollywood movies. Remarkably, his backward-looking tribute to the golden age of musicals and noirish romantic melodramas turned out to be one of his most freewheeling and personal films.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

In 1977, he directed the Broadway musical *[The Act](/wiki/The_Act_(musical) "The Act (musical)")*, starring Minnelli.[[56]](#cite_note-56) The disappointing reception of *New York, New York* drove Scorsese into depression. By this stage Scorsese had developed a serious [cocaine](/wiki/Cocaine "Cocaine") addiction. However, he did find the creative drive to make the highly regarded *[The Last Waltz](/wiki/The_Last_Waltz "The Last Waltz")*, documenting the final concert by [The Band](/wiki/The_Band "The Band"). It was held at the [Winterland Ballroom](/wiki/Winterland_Ballroom "Winterland Ballroom") in San Francisco on [Thanksgiving Day](/wiki/Thanksgiving_Day "Thanksgiving Day"), 1976, and featured one of the most extensive lineups of prominent guest performers at a single concert, including [Bob Dylan](/wiki/Bob_Dylan "Bob Dylan"), [Neil Young](/wiki/Neil_Young "Neil Young"), [Ringo Starr](/wiki/Ringo_Starr "Ringo Starr"), [Muddy Waters](/wiki/Muddy_Waters "Muddy Waters"), [Joni Mitchell](/wiki/Joni_Mitchell "Joni Mitchell"), [Van Morrison](/wiki/Van_Morrison "Van Morrison"), [Paul Butterfield](/wiki/Paul_Butterfield "Paul Butterfield"), [Neil Diamond](/wiki/Neil_Diamond "Neil Diamond"), [Ronnie Wood](/wiki/Ronnie_Wood "Ronnie Wood") and [Eric Clapton](/wiki/Eric_Clapton "Eric Clapton"). However, Scorsese's commitments to other projects delayed the release of the film until 1978. Another Scorsese-directed documentary, titled *[American Boy](/wiki/American_Boy:_A_Profile_of_Steven_Prince "American Boy: A Profile of Steven Prince")*, also appeared in 1978, focusing on Steven Prince, Scorcese's personal assistant who had a small cameo as a cocky gun salesman in *Taxi Driver*. A period of wild partying followed, damaging Scorsese's already fragile health. Scorsese helped provide footage for the documentary *[Elvis on Tour](/wiki/Elvis_on_Tour "Elvis on Tour")*.

By several accounts (Scorsese's included), De Niro saved Scorsese's life when he persuaded him to kick his [cocaine](/wiki/Cocaine "Cocaine") addiction to make his highly regarded film *[Raging Bull](/wiki/Raging_Bull "Raging Bull")*. [Mark Singer](/wiki/Mark_Singer "Mark Singer") summarized Scorsese's condition:

> He (Scorsese) was more than mildly depressed. Drug abuse, and abuse of his body in general, culminated in a terrifying episode of internal bleeding. Robert De Niro came to see him in the hospital and asked, in so many words, whether he wanted to live or die. If you want to live, De Niro proposed, let's make this picture—referring to *Raging Bull*, an as-told-to book by Jake LaMotta, the former world middleweight boxing champion, that De Niro had given him to read years earlier.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

Convinced that he would never make another movie, he poured his energies into making the violent biopic of middleweight boxing champion [Jake LaMotta](/wiki/Jake_LaMotta "Jake LaMotta"), calling it a [kamikaze](/wiki/Kamikaze "Kamikaze") method of film-making.[[58]](#cite_note-58) The film is widely viewed as a masterpiece and was voted the greatest film of the 1980s by Britain's *[Sight & Sound](/wiki/Sight_%26_Sound "Sight & Sound")* magazine.[[59]](#cite_note-59)[[60]](#cite_note-60) The film was dedicated to [Armenian-American](/wiki/Armenian-American "Armenian-American") NYU film professor [Haig P. Manoogian](/wiki/Haig_P._Manoogian "Haig P. Manoogian"), “with love and resolution”.[[61]](#cite_note-61) It received eight Oscar nominations, including Best Picture, [Best Actor](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Actor "Academy Award for Best Actor") for De Niro, [Best Supporting Actress](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Supporting_Actress "Academy Award for Best Supporting Actress") for [Cathy Moriarty](/wiki/Cathy_Moriarty "Cathy Moriarty"), [Best Supporting Actor](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Supporting_Actor "Academy Award for Best Supporting Actor") for [Joe Pesci](/wiki/Joe_Pesci "Joe Pesci") and Scorsese's first for [Best Director](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Director "Academy Award for Best Director"). De Niro won, as did Thelma Schoonmaker for editing, but Best Director went to [Robert Redford](/wiki/Robert_Redford "Robert Redford") for *[Ordinary People](/wiki/Ordinary_People "Ordinary People")*. From this work onwards, Scorsese's films are always labeled as "A Martin Scorsese Picture" on promotional material. *Raging Bull*, filmed in high contrast black and white, is where Scorsese's style reached its zenith: *Taxi Driver* and *New York, New York* had used elements of [expressionism](/wiki/Expressionism "Expressionism") to replicate psychological points of view, but here the style was taken to new extremes, employing extensive [slow-motion](/wiki/Slow-motion "Slow-motion"), complex [tracking shots](/wiki/Tracking_shot "Tracking shot"), and extravagant distortion of perspective (for example, the size of boxing rings would change from fight to fight).[[62]](#cite_note-62) Thematically too, the concerns carried on from *Mean Streets* and *Taxi Driver*: insecure males, violence, guilt, and redemption.

Although the screenplay for *Raging Bull* was credited to Paul Schrader and [Mardik Martin](/wiki/Mardik_Martin "Mardik Martin") (an Armenian-American screenwriter who earlier co-wrote *Mean Streets*), the finished script differed extensively from Schrader's original draft. It was rewritten several times by various writers including [Jay Cocks](/wiki/Jay_Cocks "Jay Cocks"). The final draft was largely written by Scorsese and De Niro.[[63]](#cite_note-63) In 1997, the [American Film Institute](/wiki/American_Film_Institute "American Film Institute") ranked *Raging Bull* as the twenty-fourth greatest American film of all time on their [AFI's 100 Years ... 100 Movies](/wiki/AFI%27s_100_Years_..._100_Movies "AFI's 100 Years ... 100 Movies") list. In 2007, they ranked *Raging Bull* as the fourth American greatest film on their [AFI's 100 Years ... 100 Movies (10th Anniversary Edition)](/wiki/AFI%27s_100_Years_..._100_Movies_(10th_Anniversary_Edition) "AFI's 100 Years ... 100 Movies (10th Anniversary Edition)") list.

Scorsese's next project was his fifth collaboration with De Niro, *[The King of Comedy](/wiki/The_King_of_Comedy_(film) "The King of Comedy (film)")* (1982). It is a [satire](/wiki/Satire "Satire") on the world of media and celebrity, whose central character is a troubled [loner](/wiki/Loner "Loner") who ironically becomes famous through a criminal act ([kidnapping](/wiki/Kidnapping "Kidnapping")).[[64]](#cite_note-64) The film was an obvious departure from the more emotionally committed films he had become associated with. Visually, it was far less kinetic than the style Scorsese had developed previously, often using a static camera and long takes.[[65]](#cite_note-65) Here the expressionism of his previous work gave way to moments of almost total [surrealism](/wiki/Surrealism "Surrealism"). It still bore many of Scorsese's trademarks, however. *The* *King of Comedy* failed at the box office, but has become increasingly well regarded by critics in the years since its release. German director [Wim Wenders](/wiki/Wim_Wenders "Wim Wenders") numbered it among his 15 favorite films.[[66]](#cite_note-66) In 1983, Scorsese made a brief [cameo appearance](/wiki/Cameo_appearance "Cameo appearance") in *[Anna Pavlova](/wiki/Anna_Pavlova_(film) "Anna Pavlova (film)")* (also known as *A Woman for All Time*), originally intended to be directed by one of his heroes, [Michael Powell](/wiki/Michael_Powell "Michael Powell"). This led to a more significant acting appearance in [Bertrand Tavernier](/wiki/Bertrand_Tavernier "Bertrand Tavernier")'s [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") film *[Round Midnight](/wiki/Round_Midnight_(film) "Round Midnight (film)")*. He also made a brief venture into television, directing an episode of [Steven Spielberg](/wiki/Steven_Spielberg "Steven Spielberg")'s *[Amazing Stories](/wiki/Amazing_Stories_(1985_TV_series) "Amazing Stories (1985 TV series)")*.

With *[After Hours](/wiki/After_Hours_(film) "After Hours (film)")* (1985), for which he won a [Best Director Award](/wiki/Cannes_Film_Festival_Award_for_Best_Director "Cannes Film Festival Award for Best Director") at Cannes, Scorsese made an esthetic shift back to a pared-down, almost "underground" film-making style. Filmed on an extremely low budget, on location, and at night in the [SoHo](/wiki/SoHo "SoHo") neighborhood of Manhattan, the film is a black comedy about one increasingly misfortunate night for a mild New York word processor ([Griffin Dunne](/wiki/Griffin_Dunne "Griffin Dunne")) and features cameos by such disparate actors as [Teri Garr](/wiki/Teri_Garr "Teri Garr") and [Cheech & Chong](/wiki/Cheech_%26_Chong "Cheech & Chong"). Along with the 1987 [Michael Jackson](/wiki/Michael_Jackson "Michael Jackson") music video "[Bad](/wiki/Bad_(Michael_Jackson_song)#Music_video "Bad (Michael Jackson song)")", in 1986 Scorsese made *[The Color of Money](/wiki/The_Color_of_Money "The Color of Money")*, a sequel to [Robert Rossen](/wiki/Robert_Rossen "Robert Rossen")'s *[The Hustler](/wiki/The_Hustler "The Hustler")* (1961) with [Paul Newman](/wiki/Paul_Newman "Paul Newman"), which co-starred [Tom Cruise](/wiki/Tom_Cruise "Tom Cruise"). Although adhering to Scorsese's established style, *The Color of Money* was Scorsese's first official foray into mainstream film-making. The film finally won Newman an Oscar and gave Scorsese the clout to finally secure backing for a project that had been a longtime goal for him: *[The Last Temptation of Christ](/wiki/The_Last_Temptation_of_Christ_(film) "The Last Temptation of Christ (film)")*.

In 1983, Scorsese began work on this long-cherished personal project. *The Last Temptation of Christ*, based on the [1955 novel](/wiki/The_Last_Temptation_of_Christ_(novel) "The Last Temptation of Christ (novel)") by [Nikos Kazantzakis](/wiki/Nikos_Kazantzakis "Nikos Kazantzakis"), retold the life of [Christ](/wiki/Christ "Christ") in human rather than divine terms. [Barbara Hershey](/wiki/Barbara_Hershey "Barbara Hershey") recalls introducing Scorsese to the book while they were filming *Boxcar Bertha*.[[67]](#cite_note-TCM-67) The film was slated to shoot under the [Paramount Pictures](/wiki/Paramount_Pictures "Paramount Pictures") banner, but shortly before principal photography was to start, Paramount pulled the plug on the project, citing pressure from religious groups. In this aborted 1983 version, [Aidan Quinn](/wiki/Aidan_Quinn "Aidan Quinn") was cast as Jesus, and [Sting](/wiki/Sting_(musician) "Sting (musician)") was cast as [Pontius Pilate](/wiki/Pontius_Pilate "Pontius Pilate"). (In the 1988 version, these roles were played by [Willem Dafoe](/wiki/Willem_Dafoe "Willem Dafoe") and [David Bowie](/wiki/David_Bowie "David Bowie") respectively.) However, following his mid-1980s flirtation with commercial Hollywood, Scorsese made a major return to personal filmmaking with the project; [Universal Pictures](/wiki/Universal_Pictures "Universal Pictures") agreed to finance the film as Scorsese agreed to make a more mainstream film for the studio in the future (it eventually resulted in *[Cape Fear](/wiki/Cape_Fear_(1991_film) "Cape Fear (1991 film)")*).[[68]](#cite_note-68) Even prior to its 1988 release, the film (adapted by *Taxi Driver* and *Raging Bull* veteran Paul Schrader) caused a massive furor, with worldwide protests against its perceived [blasphemy](/wiki/Blasphemy "Blasphemy") effectively turning a low-budget independent film into a media sensation.[[69]](#cite_note-69) Most of the controversy centered on the final passages of the film, which depicted Christ marrying and raising a family with [Mary Magdalene](/wiki/Mary_Magdalene "Mary Magdalene") in a [Satan](/wiki/Satan "Satan")-induced [hallucination](/wiki/Hallucination "Hallucination") while on the cross.

In 1986, Scorsese directed the 18-minute short film *[Bad](/wiki/Bad_(Michael_Jackson_song) "Bad (Michael Jackson song)")* featuring [Michael Jackson](/wiki/Michael_Jackson "Michael Jackson") and [Wesley Snipes](/wiki/Wesley_Snipes "Wesley Snipes") (in his film debut). The short also serves as a [music video](/wiki/Music_video "Music video") and was shot in the [Hoyt–Schermerhorn Streets station](/wiki/Hoyt%E2%80%93Schermerhorn_Streets_station "Hoyt–Schermerhorn Streets station") in [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn") over a 6-week period during November and December 1986. Chapman was the film's cinematographer. The direction and choreography were heavily influenced by *[West Side Story](/wiki/West_Side_Story_(1961_film) "West Side Story (1961 film)")* (1961). Scorsese also noted the influence of *Taxi Driver* in [Spike Lee](/wiki/Spike_Lee "Spike Lee")'s documentary *[Bad 25](/wiki/Bad_25_(film) "Bad 25 (film)")* (2012).[[70]](#cite_note-70) The short has been praised by critics as one of the greatest and most iconic videos of all time.[[71]](#cite_note-71)[[72]](#cite_note-:0-72)[[73]](#cite_note-73) That year, he had signed a deal with upstart major [The Walt Disney Studios](/wiki/Walt_Disney_Studios_(division) "Walt Disney Studios (division)") to produce and direct features, following the success of *[The Color of Money](/wiki/The_Color_of_Money "The Color of Money")*.[[74]](#cite_note-74)

Looking past the controversy, *The Last Temptation of Christ* gained critical acclaim and remains an important work in Scorsese's canon: an explicit attempt to wrestle with the spirituality underpinning his films up until that point. He received his second nomination for a Best Director Academy Award (again unsuccessfully, this time losing to [Barry Levinson](/wiki/Barry_Levinson "Barry Levinson") for *[Rain Man](/wiki/Rain_Man "Rain Man")*). Scorsese directed "Life Lessons", one of three segments in the anthology film *[New York Stories](/wiki/New_York_Stories "New York Stories")* (1989). Ebert gave the film a mixed review, while praising Scorsese's short as "really successful".[[75]](#cite_note-75)

### 1990–1999: Established director

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Martin_Scorsese&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1990–1999: Established director")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Martin_Scorsese_%282105824609%29.jpg/250px-Martin_Scorsese_%282105824609%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Martin_Scorsese_(2105824609).jpg)

Scorsese at the 1991 Academy Awards

After a decade of films considered by critics to be mixed results, some considered Scorsese's gangster epic *[Goodfellas](/wiki/Goodfellas "Goodfellas")* (1990) his return to directorial form, and his most confident and fully realized film since *Raging Bull*. De Niro and [Joe Pesci](/wiki/Joe_Pesci "Joe Pesci") offered a virtuoso display of Scorsese's bravura cinematic technique in the film and re-established, enhanced, and consolidated his reputation. After the film was released, [Roger Ebert](/wiki/Roger_Ebert "Roger Ebert"), a friend and supporter of Scorsese, named *Goodfellas* "the best mob movie ever". It is ranked No. 1 on Ebert's movie list for 1990, along with those of [Gene Siskel](/wiki/Gene_Siskel "Gene Siskel") and [Peter Travers](/wiki/Peter_Travers "Peter Travers")', and is widely considered one of the director's greatest achievements.[[76]](#cite_note-76)[[77]](#cite_note-77)[[78]](#cite_note-78) The film was nominated for six Academy Awards, including Best Picture and Best Director, and Scorsese earned his third Best Director nomination but again lost to a first-time director, [Kevin Costner](/wiki/Kevin_Costner "Kevin Costner") (*[Dances with Wolves](/wiki/Dances_with_Wolves "Dances with Wolves")*). Joe Pesci earned the [Academy Award for Best Supporting Actor](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Supporting_Actor "Academy Award for Best Supporting Actor") for his performance. Scorsese and the film also won many other awards, including five [BAFTA](/wiki/BAFTA "BAFTA") Awards, a [Silver Lion](/wiki/Silver_Lion "Silver Lion") and more. The American Film Institute placed *Goodfellas* at No. 94 on the AFI's 100 Years ... 100 Movies list. On the 2007 updated version, they moved *Goodfellas* up to No. 92 on the AFI's 100 Years ... 100 Movies list (10th Anniversary Edition) and put *Goodfellas* at No. 2 on [their list of the top 10 gangster films](/wiki/AFI%27s_10_Top_10#Gangster "AFI's 10 Top 10") (after *[The Godfather](/wiki/The_Godfather "The Godfather")*).

In 1990, he released his only short-form documentary: *[Made in Milan](/wiki/Made_in_Milan "Made in Milan")* about fashion designer [Giorgio Armani](/wiki/Giorgio_Armani "Giorgio Armani"). The following year brought *[Cape Fear](/wiki/Cape_Fear_(1991_film) "Cape Fear (1991 film)")*, a remake of a cult [1962 movie](/wiki/Cape_Fear_(1962_film) "Cape Fear (1962 film)") of the same name and the director's seventh collaboration with De Niro. Another foray into the mainstream, the film was a stylized thriller taking its cues heavily from [Alfred Hitchcock](/wiki/Alfred_Hitchcock "Alfred Hitchcock") and [Charles Laughton](/wiki/Charles_Laughton "Charles Laughton")'s *[The Night of the Hunter](/wiki/The_Night_of_the_Hunter_(film) "The Night of the Hunter (film)")* (1955). The film garnered two Oscar nominations, and earned $80 million in domestic box office, placing it as Scorsese's most commercially successful release until *The Aviator* (2004), and then *The Departed* (2006). The film also marked the first time Scorsese used wide-screen Panavision with an aspect ratio of 2.39:1.

In 1990, Scorsese acted in a small role as [Vincent van Gogh](/wiki/Vincent_van_Gogh "Vincent van Gogh") in the film *[Dreams](/wiki/Dreams_(1990_film) "Dreams (1990 film)")* by Japanese director [Akira Kurosawa](/wiki/Akira_Kurosawa "Akira Kurosawa"). Scorsese's 1994 cameo appearance in the [Robert Redford](/wiki/Robert_Redford "Robert Redford") film *[Quiz Show](/wiki/Quiz_Show_(film) "Quiz Show (film)")* is remembered for the telling line: "You see, the audience didn't tune in to watch some amazing display of intellectual ability. They just wanted to watch the money."

In 1994, Scorsese and producer Barbara De Fina formed the production company
De Fina-Cappa.[[79]](#cite_note-79) In the early 1990s, Scorsese also expanded his role as a film producer. He produced a wide range of films, including major Hollywood studio productions (*[Mad Dog and Glory](/wiki/Mad_Dog_and_Glory "Mad Dog and Glory")*, *[Clockers](/wiki/Clockers_(film) "Clockers (film)")*), low-budget independent films (*[The Grifters](/wiki/The_Grifters_(film) "The Grifters (film)")*, *[Naked in New York](/wiki/Naked_in_New_York "Naked in New York")*, *[Grace of My Heart](/wiki/Grace_of_My_Heart "Grace of My Heart")*, *[Search and Destroy](/wiki/Search_and_Destroy_(1995_film) "Search and Destroy (1995 film)")*, *[The Hi-Lo Country](/wiki/The_Hi-Lo_Country "The Hi-Lo Country")*), and even the foreign film *[Con gli occhi chiusi](/wiki/Con_gli_occhi_chiusi "Con gli occhi chiusi")* (*With Closed Eyes*).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Martin_Scorsese_03.jpg/250px-Martin_Scorsese_03.jpg)](/wiki/File:Martin_Scorsese_03.jpg)

Scorsese in 1995

*[The Age of Innocence](/wiki/The_Age_of_Innocence_(1993_film) "The Age of Innocence (1993 film)")* (1993) was a significant departure for Scorsese, a period adaptation of the [Edith Wharton](/wiki/Edith_Wharton "Edith Wharton") [novel](/wiki/The_Age_of_Innocence "The Age of Innocence") about the constrictive high society of late-19th century New York. It was highly lauded by critics upon its original release but was a [box office bomb](/wiki/Box_office_bomb "Box office bomb"), making an overall loss. As noted in *Scorsese on Scorsese* by editor–interviewer Ian Christie, the news that Scorsese wanted to make a film about a failed 19th-century romance raised many eyebrows among the film fraternity; all the more when Scorsese made it clear that it was a personal project and not a studio for-hire job.

Scorsese was interested in doing a "romantic piece", and he was strongly drawn to the characters and the story of Wharton's text. Scorsese wanted his film to be as rich an emotional experience as the book was to him rather than the traditional academic adaptations of literary works. To this end, Scorsese sought influence from diverse period films that had had an emotional impact on him. In *Scorsese on Scorsese*, he documents influences from films such as [Luchino Visconti](/wiki/Luchino_Visconti "Luchino Visconti")'s *[Senso](/wiki/Senso_(film) "Senso (film)")* and his *[Il Gattopardo](/wiki/The_Leopard_(1963_film) "The Leopard (1963 film)")* (*The Leopard*) as well as [Orson Welles](/wiki/Orson_Welles "Orson Welles")'s *[The Magnificent Ambersons](/wiki/The_Magnificent_Ambersons_(film) "The Magnificent Ambersons (film)")* and also Roberto Rossellini's *[La prise de pouvoir par Louis XIV](/wiki/La_prise_de_pouvoir_par_Louis_XIV "La prise de pouvoir par Louis XIV")* (*The Taking of Power by Louis XIV*). Although *The Age of Innocence* was ultimately different from these films in terms of narrative, story, and thematic concern, the presence of a lost society, of lost values as well as detailed re-creations of social customs and rituals continues the tradition of these films. It came back into the public eye, especially in countries such as the UK and France, but still is largely neglected in North America. The film earned five Academy Award nominations (including Best Adapted Screenplay for Scorsese), winning the Costume Design Oscar. This was his first collaboration with the Academy Award-winning actor [Daniel Day-Lewis](/wiki/Daniel_Day-Lewis "Daniel Day-Lewis"), with whom he would work again on *Gangs of New York*. This was Scorsese's first film to be shot on Super 35 format.

*[Casino](/wiki/Casino_(1995_film) "Casino (1995 film)")* (1995), like *The Age of Innocence* before it, focused on a tightly wound male whose well-ordered life is disrupted by the arrival of unpredictable forces. The fact that it was a violent gangster film made it more palatable to the director's fans who perhaps were baffled by the apparent departure of the earlier film. *Casino* was a box office success,[[80]](#cite_note-variety-gross-80) and it received generally positive notices from critics. Comparisons were drawn to his earlier film *Goodfellas*, and Scorsese admitted *Casino* bore a superficial resemblance to it, but he maintained that the story was significantly larger in scope.[[81]](#cite_note-81) [Sharon Stone](/wiki/Sharon_Stone "Sharon Stone") was nominated for the [Best Actress Academy Award](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Actress "Academy Award for Best Actress") for her performance. During the filming, Scorsese played a background part as a gambler at one of the tables.

Scorsese still found time for a four-hour documentary in 1995, titled *[A Personal Journey with Martin Scorsese Through American Movies](/wiki/A_Personal_Journey_with_Martin_Scorsese_Through_American_Movies "A Personal Journey with Martin Scorsese Through American Movies")*, offering a thorough trek through American cinema. It covered the silent era to 1969, a year after which Scorsese began his feature career. He said, "I wouldn't feel right commenting on myself or my contemporaries." In the four-hour documentary, Scorsese lists the four aspects of the director he believes are the most important as (1) the director as storyteller; (2) the director as an illusionist: D. W. Griffith or F. W. Murnau, who created new editing techniques among other innovations that made the appearance of sound and color possible later on; (3) the director as a smuggler—filmmakers such as [Douglas Sirk](/wiki/Douglas_Sirk "Douglas Sirk"), Samuel Fuller, and [Vincente Minnelli](/wiki/Vincente_Minnelli "Vincente Minnelli"), who used to hide subversive messages in their films; and (4) the director as iconoclast. In the preface to this documentary, Scorsese states his commitment to the "Director's Dilemma", in which a successful contemporary director must be pragmatic about the realities of getting financing for films of personal esthetic interest by accepting the need of "making one film for the studio, and (then) making one for oneself."

If *The Age of Innocence* alienated and confused some fans, then *[Kundun](/wiki/Kundun "Kundun")* (1997) went several steps further, offering an account of the early life of [Tenzin Gyatso, the 14th Dalai Lama](/wiki/Tenzin_Gyatso,_the_14th_Dalai_Lama "Tenzin Gyatso, the 14th Dalai Lama"), the [People's Liberation Army](/wiki/People%27s_Liberation_Army "People's Liberation Army")'s entry into [Tibet](/wiki/Tibet "Tibet"), and the Dalai Lama's subsequent exile to India. Not only a departure in subject matter, *Kundun* saw Scorsese employing a fresh narrative and visual approach. Traditional dramatic devices were substituted for a trance-like meditation achieved through an elaborate [tableau](/wiki/Tableau_vivant "Tableau vivant") of colorful visual images.[[82]](#cite_note-82) The film was a source of turmoil for its distributor, [Buena Vista Pictures](/wiki/Walt_Disney_Studios_Motion_Pictures "Walt Disney Studios Motion Pictures"), which was planning significant expansion into the Chinese market at the time. Initially defiant in the face of pressure from Chinese officials, Disney has since distanced itself from the project, hurting *Kundun*'s commercial profile. In the short term, the sheer eclecticism in evidence enhanced the director's reputation. In the long term, however, it appears *Kundun* has been sidelined in most critical appraisals of the director, mostly noted as a stylistic and thematic detour. *Kundun* was Scorsese's second attempt to profile the life of a great religious leader, following *The Last Temptation of Christ*.

*[Bringing Out the Dead](/wiki/Bringing_Out_the_Dead "Bringing Out the Dead")* (1999) was a return to familiar territory, with the director and writer Paul Schrader constructing a pitch-black comic take on their own earlier *Taxi Driver*.[[83]](#cite_note-83) Like earlier Scorsese-Schrader collaborations, its final scenes of spiritual redemption explicitly recall the films of Robert Bresson.[[84]](#cite_note-84) (It is also worth noting that the film's incident-filled nocturnal setting is reminiscent of *After Hours*.) It received generally positive reviews,[[85]](#cite_note-85) although not the universal critical acclaim of some of his other films. It stars [Nicolas Cage](/wiki/Nicolas_Cage "Nicolas Cage"), [Ving Rhames](/wiki/Ving_Rhames "Ving Rhames"), [John Goodman](/wiki/John_Goodman "John Goodman"), [Tom Sizemore](/wiki/Tom_Sizemore "Tom Sizemore"), and [Patricia Arquette](/wiki/Patricia_Arquette "Patricia Arquette").

On various occasions Scorsese has been asked to present the [Honorary Academy Award](/wiki/Honorary_Academy_Award "Honorary Academy Award") during the Oscar telecast. In 1998, at the [70th Academy Awards](/wiki/70th_Academy_Awards "70th Academy Awards"), Scorsese presented the award to film legend [Stanley Donen](/wiki/Stanley_Donen "Stanley Donen"). When accepting the award Donen quipped, "Marty this is backwards, I should be giving this to you, believe me".[[86]](#cite_note-86) In 1999, at the [71st Academy Awards](/wiki/71st_Academy_Awards "71st Academy Awards"), Scorsese and De Niro presented the award to film director [Elia Kazan](/wiki/Elia_Kazan "Elia Kazan"). This was a controversial pick for the academy due to Kazan's involvement with the [Hollywood blacklist](/wiki/Hollywood_blacklist "Hollywood blacklist") in the 1950s.[[87]](#cite_note-87) Several members of the audience including [Nick Nolte](/wiki/Nick_Nolte "Nick Nolte") and [Ed Harris](/wiki/Ed_Harris "Ed Harris") refused to applaud Kazan when he received the award while others such as [Warren Beatty](/wiki/Warren_Beatty "Warren Beatty"), [Meryl Streep](/wiki/Meryl_Streep "Meryl Streep"), [Kathy Bates](/wiki/Kathy_Bates "Kathy Bates"), and [Kurt Russell](/wiki/Kurt_Russell "Kurt Russell") gave him a standing ovation.[[88]](#cite_note-88)[[89]](#cite_note-89)

### 2000–2015: Film and television work

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Martin_Scorsese&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2000–2015: Film and television work")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Leo_Scor_Diaz%28GangsofNY%29-.jpg/250px-Leo_Scor_Diaz%28GangsofNY%29-.jpg)](/wiki/File:Leo_Scor_Diaz(GangsofNY)-.jpg)

At the *[Gangs of New York](/wiki/Gangs_of_New_York "Gangs of New York")* screening at the [2002 Cannes Film Festival](/wiki/2002_Cannes_Film_Festival "2002 Cannes Film Festival") with [Leonardo DiCaprio](/wiki/Leonardo_DiCaprio "Leonardo DiCaprio") and [Cameron Diaz](/wiki/Cameron_Diaz "Cameron Diaz")

In 1999, Scorsese directed a documentary on Italian filmmakers titled *Il Mio Viaggio in Italia*, also known as *[My Voyage to Italy](/wiki/My_Voyage_to_Italy "My Voyage to Italy")*. The documentary foreshadowed Scorsese's next project, the epic *[Gangs of New York](/wiki/Gangs_of_New_York "Gangs of New York")* (2002), influenced by (amongst many others) major Italian directors such as Luchino Visconti and filmed in its entirety at Rome's famous [Cinecittà](/wiki/Cinecitt%C3%A0 "Cinecittà") film studios. With a production budget said to be in excess of $100 million, *Gangs of New York* was Scorsese's biggest and arguably most mainstream venture to date. Like *The Age of Innocence*, it was set in 19th-century New York, although focusing on the other end of the social scale (and like that film, also starring Daniel Day-Lewis). The film marked the first collaboration between Scorsese and actor [Leonardo DiCaprio](/wiki/Leonardo_DiCaprio "Leonardo DiCaprio"), who became a fixture in later Scorsese films. The production was highly troubled, with many rumors referring to the director's conflict with [Miramax Films](/wiki/Miramax_Films "Miramax Films") boss [Harvey Weinstein](/wiki/Harvey_Weinstein "Harvey Weinstein").[[90]](#cite_note-90) The final cut of the movie ran to 168 minutes, while Scorsese's original cut was over 180 minutes long.[[91]](#cite_note-guardian_film_of_the_week-91)

Despite denials of artistic compromise, some felt that *Gangs of New York* was Scorsese's most conventional film, featuring standard film tropes that Scorsese had traditionally avoided, such as characters existing purely for [exposition](/wiki/Exposition_(literary_technique) "Exposition (literary technique)") purposes and explanatory [flashbacks](/wiki/Flashback_(literary_technique) "Flashback (literary technique)").[[91]](#cite_note-guardian_film_of_the_week-91)[[92]](#cite_note-92)[[93]](#cite_note-93) Even so, the film received generally positive reviews with the review aggregator [Rotten Tomatoes](/wiki/Rotten_Tomatoes "Rotten Tomatoes") reporting that 75 percent of the reviews for the film they tallied were positive and summarizing the critics writing, "Though flawed, the sprawling, messy *Gangs of New York* is redeemed by impressive production design and Day-Lewis's electrifying performance."[[94]](#cite_note-94) The film's central themes are consistent with Scorsese's established concerns: New York, violence as culturally endemic, and subcultural divisions down ethnic lines. Originally filmed for a release in the winter of 2001 (to qualify for Academy Award nominations), Scorsese delayed the final production of the film until after the beginning of 2002; Miramax Films consequently delayed the film until its release in the [Oscar season](/wiki/Oscar_season "Oscar season") of late 2002.[[95]](#cite_note-95) *Gangs of New York* earned Scorsese his first Golden Globe for Best Director. In February 2003, *Gangs of New York* received 10 Academy Award nominations, including Best Picture, Best Director, and Best Actor for Daniel Day-Lewis; however, it did not win in any category.

The following year, Scorsese completed production of *[The Blues](/wiki/The_Blues_(film) "The Blues (film)")*, an expansive seven-part documentary tracing the history of blues music from its African roots to the Mississippi Delta and beyond. Seven filmmakers including Wim Wenders, [Clint Eastwood](/wiki/Clint_Eastwood "Clint Eastwood"), [Mike Figgis](/wiki/Mike_Figgis "Mike Figgis"), and Scorsese himself each contributed a 90-minute film (Scorsese's entry was titled *Feel Like Going Home*). In the early 2000s, Scorsese produced several films for up-and-coming directors, such as *[You Can Count on Me](/wiki/You_Can_Count_on_Me "You Can Count on Me")* (directed by [Kenneth Lonergan](/wiki/Kenneth_Lonergan "Kenneth Lonergan")), *Rain* (directed by Katherine Lindberg), *[Lymelife](/wiki/Lymelife "Lymelife")* (directed by [Derick Martini](/wiki/Derick_Martini "Derick Martini")) and *[The Young Victoria](/wiki/The_Young_Victoria "The Young Victoria")* (directed by [Jean-Marc Vallée](/wiki/Jean-Marc_Vall%C3%A9e "Jean-Marc Vallée")). At that time, he established [Sikelia Productions](/wiki/Sikelia_Productions "Sikelia Productions").[[96]](#cite_note-96) In 2003, producer [Emma Tillinger Koskoff](/wiki/Emma_Tillinger_Koskoff "Emma Tillinger Koskoff") joined the company.[[97]](#cite_note-SD-97) Scorsese also produced several documentaries, such as *[The Soul of a Man](/wiki/The_Soul_of_a_Man "The Soul of a Man")* (directed by Wim Wenders) and *Lightning in a Bottle* (directed by [Antoine Fuqua](/wiki/Antoine_Fuqua "Antoine Fuqua")).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Martin_Scorsese_y_Leonardo_DiCaprio.jpg/250px-Martin_Scorsese_y_Leonardo_DiCaprio.jpg)](/wiki/File:Martin_Scorsese_y_Leonardo_DiCaprio.jpg)

Scorsese (left) with [Leonardo DiCaprio](/wiki/Leonardo_DiCaprio "Leonardo DiCaprio") at the 2007 [Marrakech International Film Festival](/wiki/Marrakech_International_Film_Festival "Marrakech International Film Festival")

Scorsese's film *[The Aviator](/wiki/The_Aviator_(2004_film) "The Aviator (2004 film)")* (2004) is a lavish, large-scale [biopic](/wiki/Biopic "Biopic") of eccentric aviation pioneer and film mogul [Howard Hughes](/wiki/Howard_Hughes "Howard Hughes") and reunited Scorsese with actor Leonardo DiCaprio. The film received highly positive reviews.[[98]](#cite_note-98)[[99]](#cite_note-99)[[100]](#cite_note-100)[[101]](#cite_note-101)[[102]](#cite_note-102) The film was a widespread box office success and gained Academy recognition. *The Aviator* was nominated for six [Golden Globe Awards](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Awards "Golden Globe Awards"), including [Best Motion Picture-Drama](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award_for_Best_Motion_Picture_%E2%80%93_Drama "Golden Globe Award for Best Motion Picture – Drama"), [Best Director](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award_for_Best_Director "Golden Globe Award for Best Director"), [Best Screenplay](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award_for_Best_Screenplay "Golden Globe Award for Best Screenplay"), and [Best Actor-Motion Picture Drama](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award_for_Best_Actor_%E2%80%93_Motion_Picture_Drama "Golden Globe Award for Best Actor – Motion Picture Drama") for Leonardo DiCaprio. It won three, including Best Motion Picture-Drama and Best Actor-Motion Picture Drama. In January 2005 *The Aviator* became the most-nominated film of the [77th Academy Awards](/wiki/77th_Academy_Awards "77th Academy Awards") nominations, nominated in 11 categories including Best Picture. The film also garnered nominations in nearly all the other major categories, including a fifth Best Director nomination for Scorsese. Despite having the most nominations, the film won only five Oscars. Scorsese lost again, this time to director Clint Eastwood for *[Million Dollar Baby](/wiki/Million_Dollar_Baby "Million Dollar Baby")* (which also won Best Picture).

*[No Direction Home](/wiki/No_Direction_Home "No Direction Home")* is a documentary film by Scorsese that tells of the life of Bob Dylan, and his impact on American popular music and the culture of the 20th century. The film does not cover Dylan's entire career; it focuses on his beginnings, his rise to fame in the 1960s, his then-[controversial transformation](/wiki/Electric_Dylan_controversy "Electric Dylan controversy") from an acoustic guitar-based musician and performer to an electric guitar-influenced sound and his "retirement" from touring in 1966 following an infamous motorcycle accident. The film was first presented on television in both the United States (as part of the [PBS](/wiki/PBS "PBS") *[American Masters](/wiki/American_Masters "American Masters")* series) and the United Kingdom (as part of the [BBC Two](/wiki/BBC_Two "BBC Two") *[Arena](/wiki/Arena_(UK_TV_series) "Arena (UK TV series)")* series) on September 26 to 27, 2005. A DVD version of the film was released the same month. The film won a [Peabody Award](/wiki/Peabody_Award "Peabody Award") and the [Grammy Award for Best Long Form Music Video](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_Long_Form_Music_Video "Grammy Award for Best Long Form Music Video"). In addition, Scorsese received a [Primetime Emmy Award](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award "Primetime Emmy Award") nomination for [Outstanding Directing for a Documentary/Nonfiction Program](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award_for_Outstanding_Directing_for_a_Documentary/Nonfiction_Program "Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Directing for a Documentary/Nonfiction Program"), losing to *[Baghdad ER](/wiki/Baghdad_ER "Baghdad ER")*.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Martin_Scorsese_at_the_65th_Annual_Peabody_Awards.jpg/250px-Martin_Scorsese_at_the_65th_Annual_Peabody_Awards.jpg)](/wiki/File:Martin_Scorsese_at_the_65th_Annual_Peabody_Awards.jpg)

Scorsese at the 65th Annual Peabody Awards in mid-2006

Scorsese returned to the crime genre with the Boston-set thriller *[The Departed](/wiki/The_Departed "The Departed")*, based on the Hong Kong police drama *[Infernal Affairs](/wiki/Infernal_Affairs "Infernal Affairs")* (which is co-directed by [Andrew Lau](/wiki/Andrew_Lau "Andrew Lau") and [Alan Mak](/wiki/Alan_Mak_(director) "Alan Mak (director)")). The film continued Scorsese's collaboration with Leonardo DiCaprio and was the first time he worked with [Matt Damon](/wiki/Matt_Damon "Matt Damon"), [Jack Nicholson](/wiki/Jack_Nicholson "Jack Nicholson"), [Mark Wahlberg](/wiki/Mark_Wahlberg "Mark Wahlberg"), and [Martin Sheen](/wiki/Martin_Sheen "Martin Sheen"). *The Departed* opened to widespread critical acclaim, with some proclaiming it as one of the best efforts Scorsese had brought to the screen since 1990's *Goodfellas*,[[103]](#cite_note-103)[[104]](#cite_note-104) and still others putting it at the same level as Scorsese's most celebrated classics *Taxi Driver* and *Raging Bull*.[[105]](#cite_note-105)[[106]](#cite_note-106) With domestic box office receipts surpassing [US$](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar")129.4 million, *The Departed* was Scorsese's highest-grossing film (not accounting for inflation) until 2010's *[Shutter Island](/wiki/Shutter_Island_(film) "Shutter Island (film)")*. *The Departed* earned Scorsese a second Golden Globe for Best Director, as well as a Critics' Choice Award, his first [Directors Guild of America](/wiki/Directors_Guild_of_America "Directors Guild of America") Award, and the Academy Award for Best Director. Presented with the latter, Scorsese poked fun at his track record of nominations, asking, "Could you double-check the envelope?"[[107]](#cite_note-107) The award was presented by his longtime friends and colleagues [Francis Ford Coppola](/wiki/Francis_Ford_Coppola "Francis Ford Coppola"), [George Lucas](/wiki/George_Lucas "George Lucas") and [Steven Spielberg](/wiki/Steven_Spielberg "Steven Spielberg"). *The Departed* also received the Academy Award for the Best Motion Picture of 2006, Best Adapted Screenplay, and Best Film Editing by longtime Scorsese editor Thelma Schoonmaker, her third win for a Scorsese film.

*[Shine a Light](/wiki/Shine_a_Light_(film) "Shine a Light (film)")* captures [rock and roll](/wiki/Rock_and_roll "Rock and roll") band [The Rolling Stones](/wiki/The_Rolling_Stones "The Rolling Stones")' performing at New York City's [Beacon Theatre](/wiki/Beacon_Theatre_(New_York_City) "Beacon Theatre (New York City)") on October 29 and November 1, 2006, intercut with brief news and interview footage from throughout their career. The film was initially scheduled for release on September 21, 2007, but [Paramount Classics](/wiki/Paramount_Classics "Paramount Classics") postponed its general release until April 2008. Its world premiere was at the opening of the 58th [Berlinale](/wiki/Berlinale "Berlinale") Film Festival on February 7, 2008. "Marty did an amazing job of making us look great ..." observed drummer [Charlie Watts](/wiki/Charlie_Watts "Charlie Watts"). "It's all in the edits and the cuts. That's a movie maker rather than a guy just shooting a band onstage ... It's not *[Casablanca](/wiki/Casablanca_(film) "Casablanca (film)")*, but it's a great thing to have from our point of view, not being egotistical. It's a document."[[108]](#cite_note-108)

In 2009, Scorsese signed a petition in support of director [Roman Polanski](/wiki/Roman_Polanski "Roman Polanski"), who had been detained while traveling to a film festival in relation to his 1977 [sexual abuse charges](/wiki/Roman_Polanski_sexual_abuse_case "Roman Polanski sexual abuse case"), which the petition argued would undermine the tradition of film festivals as a place for works to be shown "freely and safely", and that arresting filmmakers traveling to neutral countries could open the door "for actions of which no-one can know the effects."[[109]](#cite_note-109)[[110]](#cite_note-110)

On October 22, 2007, *[Daily Variety](/wiki/Daily_Variety "Daily Variety")* reported that Scorsese would reunite with Leonardo DiCaprio on a fourth picture, *Shutter Island*. Principal photography on the Laeta Kalogridis screenplay, based on the novel of the same name by [Dennis Lehane](/wiki/Dennis_Lehane "Dennis Lehane"), began in [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts") in March 2008.[[111]](#cite_note-111)[[112]](#cite_note-112) In December 2007, actors [Mark Ruffalo](/wiki/Mark_Ruffalo "Mark Ruffalo"), [Max von Sydow](/wiki/Max_von_Sydow "Max von Sydow"), [Ben Kingsley](/wiki/Ben_Kingsley "Ben Kingsley"), and [Michelle Williams](/wiki/Michelle_Williams_(actress) "Michelle Williams (actress)") joined the cast,[[113]](#cite_note-113)[[114]](#cite_note-114) marking the first time these actors had worked with Scorsese. The film was released on February 19, 2010.[[115]](#cite_note-115) On May 20, 2010, *Shutter Island* became Scorsese's highest-grossing film.[[116]](#cite_note-116) In 2010, *[The Wall Street Journal](/wiki/The_Wall_Street_Journal "The Wall Street Journal")* reported that Scorsese was supporting the [David Lynch Foundation](/wiki/David_Lynch_Foundation "David Lynch Foundation")'s initiative to help 10,000 military veterans overcome [posttraumatic stress disorder](/wiki/Posttraumatic_stress_disorder "Posttraumatic stress disorder") through [Transcendental Meditation](/wiki/Transcendental_Meditation_technique "Transcendental Meditation technique");[[117]](#cite_note-117) Scorsese has publicly discussed his own practice of TM.[[118]](#cite_note-118)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/Martin_Scorsese_Cannes_2010.jpg/250px-Martin_Scorsese_Cannes_2010.jpg)](/wiki/File:Martin_Scorsese_Cannes_2010.jpg)

Scorsese at Cannes in 2010

Scorsese directed a television commercial for [Chanel](/wiki/Chanel "Chanel")'s then-new men's fragrance, *[Bleu de Chanel](/wiki/Bleu_de_Chanel "Bleu de Chanel")*, starring French actor [Gaspard Ulliel](/wiki/Gaspard_Ulliel "Gaspard Ulliel"). Filmed in New York City, it debuted online on August 25, 2010, and was released on TV in September 2010.[[119]](#cite_note-119) Scorsese directed [the series premiere](/wiki/Pilot_(Boardwalk_Empire) "Pilot (Boardwalk Empire)") for *[Boardwalk Empire](/wiki/Boardwalk_Empire "Boardwalk Empire")*, an HBO drama series,[[120]](#cite_note-120) starring [Steve Buscemi](/wiki/Steve_Buscemi "Steve Buscemi") and [Michael Pitt](/wiki/Michael_Pitt "Michael Pitt"), based on Nelson Johnson's book *Boardwalk Empire: The Birth, High Times and Corruption of Atlantic City*.[[121]](#cite_note-boardwalk-121) [Terence Winter](/wiki/Terence_Winter "Terence Winter"), who wrote for *[The Sopranos](/wiki/The_Sopranos "The Sopranos")*, created the series. In addition to directing the pilot (for which he won the 2011 Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Directing), Scorsese also served as an executive producer on the series.[[121]](#cite_note-boardwalk-121) The series premiered on September 19, 2010, and ran for five seasons.[[121]](#cite_note-boardwalk-121)

Scorsese directed the three-and-a-half-hour documentary *[George Harrison: Living in the Material World](/wiki/George_Harrison:_Living_in_the_Material_World "George Harrison: Living in the Material World")* about the life and music of former [Beatles](/wiki/Beatles "Beatles")' member [George Harrison](/wiki/George_Harrison "George Harrison"), which premiered in the United States on [HBO](/wiki/HBO "HBO") over two parts on October 5 and 6, 2011.[[122]](#cite_note-122) His next film *[Hugo](/wiki/Hugo_(film) "Hugo (film)")* is a [3D](/wiki/3D_film "3D film") [adventure](/wiki/Adventure_film "Adventure film") [drama film](/wiki/Drama_film "Drama film") based on [Brian Selznick](/wiki/Brian_Selznick "Brian Selznick")'s novel *[The Invention of Hugo Cabret](/wiki/The_Invention_of_Hugo_Cabret "The Invention of Hugo Cabret")*. The film stars [Asa Butterfield](/wiki/Asa_Butterfield "Asa Butterfield"), [Chloë Grace Moretz](/wiki/Chlo%C3%AB_Grace_Moretz "Chloë Grace Moretz"), Ben Kingsley, [Sacha Baron Cohen](/wiki/Sacha_Baron_Cohen "Sacha Baron Cohen"), [Ray Winstone](/wiki/Ray_Winstone "Ray Winstone"), [Emily Mortimer](/wiki/Emily_Mortimer "Emily Mortimer"), [Christopher Lee](/wiki/Christopher_Lee "Christopher Lee"), and [Jude Law](/wiki/Jude_Law "Jude Law"). The film has been met with critical acclaim[[123]](#cite_note-123)[[124]](#cite_note-124) and earned Scorsese his third [Golden Globe Award](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award "Golden Globe Award") for Best Director. The film was also nominated for 11 Academy Awards, winning five of them and becoming tied with [Michel Hazanavicius](/wiki/Michel_Hazanavicius "Michel Hazanavicius")'s film [*The Artist*](/wiki/The_Artist_(film) "The Artist (film)") for the most Academy Awards won by a single film in 2011. *Hugo* also won two [BAFTA](/wiki/BAFTA "BAFTA") awards, among [numerous other awards and nominations](/wiki/Hugo_(film)#Accolades "Hugo (film)"). *Hugo* was Scorsese's first [3D](/wiki/3-D_film "3-D film") film and was released in the United States on November 23, 2011.[[125]](#cite_note-ReleaseDates-125)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Martin_Scorsese_Tribeca_2007_Shankbone.jpg/250px-Martin_Scorsese_Tribeca_2007_Shankbone.jpg)](/wiki/File:Martin_Scorsese_Tribeca_2007_Shankbone.jpg)

At the [Tribeca Film Festival](/wiki/Tribeca_Film_Festival "Tribeca Film Festival") in 2007

Scorsese's 2013 film, *[The Wolf of Wall Street](/wiki/The_Wolf_of_Wall_Street_(2013_film) "The Wolf of Wall Street (2013 film)")*,[[126]](#cite_note-126) is an American [biographical](/wiki/Biographical_film "Biographical film") [black comedy](/wiki/Black_comedy "Black comedy") based on [Jordan Belfort](/wiki/Jordan_Belfort "Jordan Belfort")'s memoir of the same name. The screenplay was written by Terence Winter and starred Leonardo DiCaprio as Belfort, along with [Jonah Hill](/wiki/Jonah_Hill "Jonah Hill"), [Matthew McConaughey](/wiki/Matthew_McConaughey "Matthew McConaughey"), and others. The film marked the fifth collaboration between Scorsese and DiCaprio and the second between Scorsese and Winter after *Boardwalk Empire*. It was released on December 25, 2013. The film tells the story of a New York stockbroker, played by DiCaprio, who engages in a large [securities fraud](/wiki/Securities_fraud "Securities fraud") case involving illicit stock manipulation, by way of the practice of "[pump and dump](/wiki/Pump_and_dump "Pump and dump")". DiCaprio was given the award for [Best Actor-Motion Picture Musical or Comedy](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award_for_Best_Actor_%E2%80%93_Motion_Picture_Musical_or_Comedy "Golden Globe Award for Best Actor – Motion Picture Musical or Comedy") at the [2014 Golden Globe Awards](/wiki/2014_Golden_Globe_Awards "2014 Golden Globe Awards"). The film was also nominated for [Best Motion Picture-Musical or Comedy](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award_for_Best_Motion_Picture_%E2%80%93_Musical_or_Comedy "Golden Globe Award for Best Motion Picture – Musical or Comedy") as well. *The Wolf of Wall Street* was nominated for five Academy Awards, including Best Picture, Best Actor for Leonardo DiCaprio, Best Supporting Actor for Jonah Hill, Best Director for Martin Scorsese, and [Best Adapted Screenplay](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Adapted_Screenplay "Academy Award for Best Adapted Screenplay") for Terence Winter but did not win in any category.[[127]](#cite_note-127) In a 2016 critics' poll conducted by the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC"), the film was ranked among the 100 greatest motion pictures since 2000.[[128]](#cite_note-128)

Scorsese and David Tedeschi made a documentary about the history of *[The New York Review of Books](/wiki/The_New_York_Review_of_Books "The New York Review of Books")*, titled *[The 50 Year Argument](/wiki/The_50_Year_Argument "The 50 Year Argument")*. It screened as a work in progress at the [Berlin International Film Festival](/wiki/Berlin_International_Film_Festival "Berlin International Film Festival") in February 2014 and premiered in June 2014 at the [Sheffield DocFest](/wiki/Sheffield_DocFest "Sheffield DocFest").[[129]](#cite_note-129)[[130]](#cite_note-130) It was also screened in [Oslo](/wiki/Oslo "Oslo"),[[131]](#cite_note-131) and [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem_Film_Festival "Jerusalem Film Festival")[[132]](#cite_note-132) before being shown on the BBC's *Arena* series in July[[133]](#cite_note-133) and at [Telluride](/wiki/Telluride_Film_Festival "Telluride Film Festival") in August.[[134]](#cite_note-134) In September, it was screened at the [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto_International_Film_Festival "Toronto International Film Festival")[[135]](#cite_note-135) and [Calgary International Film Festivals](/wiki/Calgary_International_Film_Festival "Calgary International Film Festival"),[[136]](#cite_note-136) and the [New York Film Festival](/wiki/New_York_Film_Festival "New York Film Festival").[[137]](#cite_note-137) It aired on HBO on September 29, 2014.[[138]](#cite_note-138)
